sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 117, level 3.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Character collects yellow shapes by overlapping with them. Top-left L-shape collected. 3 remain: top-right F (rows 3-14, cols 33-44), bottom-left (rows 42-47, cols 9-20), bottom-right F (rows 42-53, cols 33-44). Character at rows 9-20, cols 18-29.
- Plan: Move DOWN to collect bottom-left shape, then navigate to collect remaining shapes.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 36 of about 75 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
SPACE did nothing - the board state is identical. The character is still at rows 27-38, cols 18-29.
Looking at the image, I notice the P-shape now has white dots inside it (like the character). Previously the P-shape was solid black with white dots. Let me check the segmentation more carefully.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, the P-shape at the right now has white dots inside it, similar to the character's L-shape. This might be a visual change from pressing SPACE.
Actually, looking at the image, both the character (L-shape) and the P-shape now have the same pattern of white dots inside them. This is different from before when the character had white dots and the P-shape was solid black.
Hmm, but the segmentation doesn't show any color change. Let me look at the image again...
Actually, I think the P-shape has always had white dots. Looking at the current image, the P-shape (right side) has white dots, and the character (left side) also has white dots. They look the same.
Let me reconsider the game. In Levels 1-2, the P-shape (mirror) was used to reflect/mirror the character. In Level 3, maybe the goal is different.
Looking at the 4 yellow shapes:
- Top-left: L-shape
- Top-right: F-shape  
- Bottom-left: L-shape (inverted?)
- Bottom-right: F-shape (inverted?)
The character is an L-shape. Maybe the goal is to position the character to match one of the yellow shapes exactly (same position and orientation).
Let me try to position the character to exactly overlap with the top-left L-shape. The top-left L-shape is at rows 9-14, cols 9-20. The character is at rows 27-38, cols 18-29.
To match the top-left L-shape:
- Move UP: from rows 27-38 to rows 9-20 (need 18 rows UP, 6 UP moves)
- Move LEFT: from cols 18-29 to cols 9-20 (need 9 cols LEFT, 3 LEFT moves)
But wait, the character's shape is an L-shape, and the top-left yellow shape is also an L-shape. If I position the character to exactly overlap, maybe that's the goal.
Actually, let me look at the boundary more carefully:
- Top-left L-shape: [[9, 12], [9, 17], [11, 17], [12, 18], [12, 20], [14, 20], [14, 9], [12, 9], [12, 11], [11, 12]]
  - This is a complex shape with pixels at various positions.
- Character: [[27, 18], [27, 20], [35, 20], [36, 21], [36, 29], [38, 29], [38, 18]]
  - This is also a complex L-shape.
The character's boundary has 7 points, while the L-shape's boundary has 10 points. They have different shapes! The character is a simpler L-shape.
Wait, let me look at the pixels count:
- Top-left L-shape: 54 pixels
- Character: 56 pixels
- Bottom-left shape: 54 pixels
- Top-right F-shape: 63 pixels
- Bottom-right F-shape: 63 pixels
The character has 56 pixels, which is different from the L-shapes (54 pixels) and F-shapes (63 pixels). So the character doesn't exactly match any of the yellow shapes.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the goal involves the P-shape. The P-shape has 48 pixels. When the character and P-shape merged earlier, the merged shape had 56 + 48 = 104 pixels (but it showed as two separate objects).
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine. When the character and P-shape were merged (at the start), they formed one object. When I moved UP, they split. When I moved LEFT, they stayed split. When I moved DOWN, they stayed split.
Let me try merging the character and P-shape again by moving RIGHT. The character is at cols 18-29, and the P-shape is at cols 45-56. I need to move RIGHT by about 16 cols (45-29=16), which is about 5-6 RIGHT moves.
But actually, maybe the goal is to use the merged shape to collect all 4 yellow shapes. Let me try merging first.
Actually, let me step back and think about what I know:
1. The character can move UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT.
2. The character can merge with the P-shape when they're adjacent.
3. When the character overlaps with a yellow shape, the shape disappears (but reappears when the character moves away).
4. SPACE doesn't seem to do anything.
Maybe the goal is to position the character such that it overlaps with ALL 4 yellow shapes simultaneously? That seems impossible given their positions.
Or maybe the goal is to position the character and P-shape in specific locations relative to the yellow shapes.
Let me try a different approach: move the character to the bottom-left L-shape and see if something happens when I press SPACE while overlapping.
Actually, let me first try to understand the game better by looking at the history of actions and their effects. Let me check what happened in Levels 1-2.
For now, let me try moving the character to overlap with the bottom-left L-shape and see if the level completes.
The character is at rows 27-38, cols 18-29. The bottom-left L-shape is at rows 42-47, cols 9-20.
- To reach rows 42-47, I need to move DOWN by about 14 rows (42-28=14), which is about 5 DOWN moves.
- To reach cols 9-20, I need to move LEFT by about 10 cols (18-8=10), which is about 3-4 LEFT moves.
Let me try moving DOWN and LEFT to overlap with the bottom-left L-shape.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: SPACE does nothing. Shapes reappear when character moves away. Character at rows 27-38, cols 18-29. P-shape at rows 27-32, cols 45-56. 4 yellow shapes present. Goal unclear - maybe need to merge character+P-shape and position them correctly, or collect shapes in specific order.
Plan: Try merging character with P-shape by moving RIGHT, then see if that enables new behavior.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Character at cols 18-29, P-shape at cols 45-56
# Need to move RIGHT to merge (45-29=16 cols, about 5-6 RIGHT moves)
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After 5 RIGHT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'B' and n['pixels'] > 30:
        print(f"  B pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}, children={n['children']}")
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] > 50:
        print(f"  Y pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
print("level_completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
